speaker: interviewer
text: I'd rather not. I'd rather let them talk constantly, but you have to ask questions so they can tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: But each-- each person--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Oh, no. I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Takes it in--

speaker: interviewer
text: --all differently. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they won't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely. With details. And some people just didn't-- he didn't want to say any details. He's very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yesterday, a gentleman-- seven tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewer
text: We ran out of tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think the only-- were you with me, Kevin?

speaker: interviewer
text: His wife was six, and he was seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? You're kidding? No kidding. Were you with me when I was in Santa Monica, when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Where the-- what's his name? [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, he went like five tapes, right? He went five tapes, and she did like in three tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Right. OK. We are rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were ready to leave the displaced person camp. Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the 31st of August, I came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you had family here in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because my uncles all lived here, you know, and the family. And plus, the fact that I always had the desire as a young fellow to come to America, because my mother used to talk so much about New York where she lived and she went to school as a young girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you something before you go to America. When you went back to Czechoslovakia with your two sisters, how were you treated there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't treated very nice. And our neighbors who practically used to live in our house, in my mother's house was always-- her door was always open to help anybody. She was that type of a person. And the same people came and told us that too bad that Hitler left a few of us to hate us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say that again. That Hitler said what?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's too bad that a few of you left, that Hitler left a few of you to hate us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was astonished. I didn't know what to answer. I couldn't believe that they would say something like that. I mean, I thought that when we come out of the camp and we're going to walk on the street, everybody go bow front of us. That couldn't believe that a human being can survive such a suffering what I endured, what I witnessed, what I had seen. So to me, it was unbelievable that-- that people still had that much hatred and that much discrimination. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very difficult to accept, very difficult. It took me month, and month, and month until I came to myself when I came to America. I mean, I told that, you know, that-- learn that if you smile, the world smiles with you. When you cry, you cry alone. So I learned not to talk about my experience to people and just to have pleasant things to talk about. Because it took me a long time to learn that if people didn't experience the same thing what I experienced, it's-- they're not going to believe it. They won't believe it. Where did you first go to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed with my aunt for three days. And soon as I arrived, I wanted to work. I wanted to have a job. So I didn't have a trade. Even that I had a little training in cosmetology in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my aunt friend had known a place which was an international beauty shop. They had a lot of people there, about 42 people exact worked there. It called, Madame Fisher's Beauty Salon. It was the shop in New York, 719 Lexington Avenue.

speaker: interviewee
text: A matter of fact, my boss, his nephew was Mr. Schlesinger, the one who worked for the government while I was there. What's his name now. I know it's Schlesinger. I know-- I don't remember the first name. He was in the State Department something, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took me there because I-- they knew that one of the language that I spoke, I would be able to get by. So as I said, I was three days in New York. And they took me to this shop. And I had learned my trade right in the shop instead of going to a trade school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was so good at that that even that she had-- she had a lot of the-- the everybody who was somebody in New York, it was in that shop by Madame Fisher. She was a very talented lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I sat there, and watched her, and watched her, and I did everything. I was taken in as an errand boy. I was paid about $24, some cents a week. And as I said, I stayed two weeks with my aunt. And then I-- this Madame Fisher had a customer who's daughter got married, and she took me in. She says, would you take this young man in to live with you? So she had a room. She never had a border before, so she gave me room and board for $9 a week. And you didn't speak English by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke very little English, just a few words. But I was able to get by because-- because there was other people who spoke other languages. You know, I spoke very good German at that time. And there were some German fellows who worked and some Hungarians, so I got by. And I'd picked up the trade so fast. And as I said, that she had very high-class clientele. She had like movie stars-- May West, Nancy Carroll, the singer at that time, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Gabor family, which you know even today. [LAUGHS] Momma Gabor and the three daughters. It was very interesting. And I practically took over. I stole her customer, my boss' customer, I became so good. And I stayed there for about three and a half years, approximately. And then I came to California.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to California in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I meet my wife in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a temple-- in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what kind of a synagogue? On Sunset Boulevard. That's west of the freeway. What was the name of that synagogue? [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can't think of the name. Anyway, that's where I met her, in a synagogue there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long after that did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got married-- we met in May, and we got married in November. Six months, not quite.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, is your wife an American?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she's a native Californian.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. She was born in Long Beach during the earthquake. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I have children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two-- I have two daughters. The first one is Wendy and the second one is Laurie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they both married?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're both married, and they have children. My oldest one, Wendy, has two children. Steven-- by the name-- the oldest one is Steven. And the youngest one is Danny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still working, or are you retired now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I am retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're retired now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: I hear that we're going to have your two daughters, and your wife, and some of your grandchildren with you, and we're going to take photos of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said that they're coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: William, tell me. Do still think about the Holocaust often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I try not to because if I do think about it, you know-- I mean, I had for many, many years, I had terrible dreams all the time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the terrible things. You know, woke up that-- [SIGHS] they're beating me or the SS is coming after me. And the concentration camp, and my parents, and everything. But I thought about it and all the family who I lost. So-- so I've sort of blocked out, you know. Trying to block it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's just destiny in life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's unbelievable because the places where I've been, and what I had lived through, and all the people who I seen dying, you know. That as-- as I said to you before, that-- that mountains where we were working under the tunnels, there was about 600 of us taken there. And typhus epidemic came and took practically all of them. And I think about 60, 62 was left alive, and I was one of it. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of message would you like to leave for your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just hope that-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] that history doesn't repeat itself. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CRYING] And they can live and let live in peace. And they have a nice future. And all this hate that will die.

speaker: interviewer
text: William, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. We can stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm June Harvey, the wife of William Harvey. And I'd also like to introduce you to my other family. This is my lovely grandson, Steven. And another one, Daniel. And my daughter, Wendy and Laurie.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're seven and going on nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to say that it's April 28th, 1995. OK. Tell me, how much did William talk to you about his experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little about his past. I asked him many times, and he didn't want to really discussing it in detail. Just a very few details of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it because it was painful or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I imagine it's painful. And I know from my new experiences that he blocks things out that are painful. You know, it makes it easier for him. And also he told me the way he, you know, was able to survive afterwards, you know, in this country was to block the painful experiences out. And there were many of them, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, are you kind of glad that he gave his testimony today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because I've been wanting him to talk to somebody, but he would never go to a psychologist with me. I thought it would be good for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But doesn't he go into the Jewish organization? Like the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the Guardians.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the Guardian organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it just supports the Jewish Home for the Aged in Reseda.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the only one he belongs to.

speaker: interviewer
text: But isn't he involved with the Simon Wiesenthal Center?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: More of a donor.

speaker: interviewer
text: More of a donor. But he just--

speaker: interviewee
text: He just goes to some of their affairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Let me talk to-- I might as well-- to Laurie, who's next to you. How do you feel about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he's the best. Nobody comes close to my dad. He's one in a million.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, don't be that general. He's the best.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he is. He is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then tell me, why is he the best?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's the most giving person. Never expects in return. And it's not just with his family, it's with everybody. He's just special.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel that-- we just talked to him. He was very emotional during the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is, I think, very, very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: How can you not be?

speaker: interviewer
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: How can you not be?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Get close to the mic. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No. I mean, it was an emotional event. I think everybody is.

speaker: interviewer
text: But are you also glad that he's leaving his testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. I have a 2 1/2-year-old who's not here today. And he adores his grandfather. And to have this memorialized on tape is very special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful. Do you want to pass the microphone to Wendy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a long way there. Wendy, you're the youngest one or you're the oldest. I beg your pardon. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Keep going. Keep going. I like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't mean it that you're younger than Laurie. No, you look older than Laurie. [LAUGHS] I'm kidding. [LAUGHTER]

words.text: it that you're younger than Laurie.

speaker: interviewee
text: You made her day. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: What did you want to know?

words.text: What did you want to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: You said the magic words.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just wanted to make you very happy. That's all. Tell me about your father. Tell me what you really-- makes you happy, or sad about him or what.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just think it's awful what he had to go through, you know, and experience. And it's awful that, you know, the hate that he felt, unfortunately, is still alive today. I felt it when I was working, when I was younger. And we had been getting hate literature in our mailbox. Like last week we had to call the police out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of hate letters were you getting?

speaker: interviewee
text: The month before, it was against Hispanics and African Americans. This month it was against us. Specifically, against Jewish women, and it was very graphic. So I thought I was being targeted. But I found that it ranged about five blocks in our neighborhood, so it wasn't just against me. So it's really very sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now let me ask you, as a second generation survivor that you are, how did he raise you? I mean, I know, I just heard that he blocked things out of his life. But did it affect you badly or did you ask your father about why don't I have a grandmother, and a grandfather, and all these things that other children of my age have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew. We knew he was a Holocaust survivor. As far as asking him, you felt that he didn't want to speak about it. So until I was about 35, he never really spoke about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did it effect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How can I explain it? It's just something you feel, and you become very sensitive to the hate that's out there. It's just-- you know, you're just very sensitized. And I don't know what it is. It just makes you very aware, unfortunately, of the evil, the bad side of human beings. But it's just something you feel. It's not something that he ever explained to me. He never explained to me to discriminate, to hate anything like that, or to be aware of how people can have all those terrible emotions towards you. I never experienced that till I started working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Working where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was an escrow officer when I started out. And the people I went to lunch with every day-- one day after Easter, the person who was training me to be the escrow officer just came at me with fangs. You know, you're a Christ killer and all this stuff. I guess they must have seen one of those passion plays at church or something. But it was just-- I could never understand the depth of hate that my father felt till that experience. It was just unbelievable. And at that time, I was just 22. I didn't realize that the whole office was Catholic, and they were all just very hateful to me and jealous of my position in the company. But I didn't realize it was a religious thing. I thought maybe it was just jealousy with my position. But unfortunately, it was that religious thing, because the lady just jumped at me. Her fangs were at my throat. It was just awful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I started asking my dad some questions gradually. Are you a California born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So is your sister, Laurie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were raised in this house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? Now tell me, as a second generation survivor, are you involved with any second generation organizations or to keep on-- prayer. Bringing the Holocaust alive and to see to it that the revisionists don't deny it anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But as an adult-- because I survived a life-threatening disease-- I sort of wanted to find the answers on my own religion. And since I experienced-- gradually as I got older and started experience, I guess, a lot of hate because of my religion--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. And I first started studying yoga and meditation, but I realized that wasn't everything. It was just a tool. So anyways, I started taking a lot of Judaic courses. And I was interested in finding out where did this anti-Semitism come from. But instead, I started focusing on the beauty of Judaism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those are your children, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- let me talk to the youngest one there. Is he the youngest one? Yes. He's only eight years old now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep it close.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: He will. They're in show business already. Anyway, how do you feel about your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. Because I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of what he went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what he went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it make you sad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you going to see that it doesn't happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good for you. Let me talk to your brother who's a year older than you. I'm going to ask you the same question. How do you feel about your grandfather and what he went through all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I feel very happy for him because he survived the Holocaust. But then I also feel sad for him because he had to go through it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'll see to it that nothing like this happens so it won't happen to you either?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it can happen to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes? And you'll do something about it, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Just hand the microphone to your grandmother. I think your grandfather has talked enough already. [LAUGHTER] No. Give it back to him-- one second to William.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you feel about your family.

speaker: interviewer
text: He is miked. He is miked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he is miked. You don't need that. You don't need a mic. You can give it to your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. How I feel about my family?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no word can express it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, try to use your words.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a wonderful family. I have two wonderful daughters and three wonderful grandchildrens. And ever I want to think about some bad things, I think about them. Then I forget about the bad things. And if ever I want to forget about the bad things, I go over to their house, and I spend a day with them, and then I forget about everything. And I have nothing but joy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you have a wonderful family, and I hope this testimony will help the future generations to be better citizens. Thank you very much, all of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to show photos now.

speaker: interviewer
text: He should mention--

speaker: interviewer
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: He's miked. So just tell them that, now I'm going to show the photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. About the photos?

speaker: interviewer
text: You should mention that we're going to show photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say it. You're miked already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dad, you have it on your front.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't need it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now, we're going to show the photo of my past.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell me who that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me when I four, not quite five years old. And it was taken around 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berehovo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1929, approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next. What are you going to say? I'm the one with the white shirt and the big bow tie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the one who's laying on the floor with the white shirt and the bow tie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where was that taken? I see a lot of children there.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in the second grade, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? In Berehovo, Czechoslovakia in my first grade or second grade class.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said it was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been, let's see, about 1931.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken 1940, and my mother is sitting down.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name was Zali Herskovits. H-E-R-S-K-O-V-I-T-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is sitting next to her? The woman next to her on her left?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister. To her left, is a friend of ours. And to the right, is my sister, Margaret. And I am back of my mother standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say it was-- in what year was it? 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in 1940. And it was taken in Czechoslovakia in a back yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still the same city?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the same city under a different regime, [RUSSIAN], Russia. [RUSSIAN], Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me again. This picture taken around 1938. And this was taken in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berehovo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Berehovo, 1938. You were born in 1924. Let them count.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 13 years old. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Is that when you did your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, around that time. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good looking guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: To my left, my mother is sitting and-- to her right--

speaker: interviewer
text: Above her, who is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Above to the left is my sister, Margaret. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to my mother is sitting my sister, Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And next to your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to my sister, I am standing. And this picture was taken also in Berehovo in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. And now, tell me if the uncle is on your father or mother's side? That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Ready? We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my aunt and uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Rose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my uncle's name was Isador Weis. And he's a brother to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was-- must have been taken in the '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In California. They in lived here in California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my brother, Gilbert, who was also born in Czechoslovakia, Berehovo. And this picture was taken around, I believe, about '37, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clear.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here we go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my sister, Margaret, and Frank. And they were married in Deggendorf, Niederbayern in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's when you went into a displacement camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Displacement camp, yeah. That's the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. That's in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany. And my brother-in-law also had a family before that who he lost in a concentration camp-- two children and his wife.

speaker: interviewer
